full_response:
Let  
L = {l1 , … , ln } , li = (xi , yi ) with ‑1 ≤ xi ≤ 1 and all yi ’s distinct.  
The points are already given in increasing y–order:
y1 < y2 < … < yn .
Moreover, after the cheap “½ × ½–square test’’ done in part (a) we know

(†)  every axis-parallel square of side ½ contains at most one launch-site.

Goal Decide whether ∃ i ≠ j such that ‖li – lj‖ < 1 in worst–case Θ(n) time
(not counting the presorting).

----------------------------------------------------
1 Geometric facts
----------------------------------------------------
If two points are closer than 1, their y-coordinates differ by < 1:
 ‖li – lj‖ < 1 ⇒ |yi – yj| < 1.                    (1)

Hence, when we scan the points in y-order, any new point needs to be
compared only with sites whose y–coordinate lies in the vertical interval
[yi – 1 , yi ].

Next split the strip –1 ≤ x ≤ 1 into four vertical slabs of width ½ :

S0 :  –1 ≤ x < –½    
S1 :  –½ ≤ x <    0    
S2 :     0 ≤ x <  ½    
S3 :   ½ ≤ x ≤   1

Inside the vertical window of height 1 that we have to watch
(inequality (1)),
there are at most two horizontal “rows’’ of height ½ each.
Together with the four slabs we obtain at most 2 × 4 = 8
axis-parallel ½ × ½ squares.

Because of property (†) each of these eight squares contains **at most one
point**.  Consequently

(*) At any moment there are ≤ 8 candidate points that might be within
    distance < 1 of the current point.

----------------------------------------------------
2 Algorithm
----------------------------------------------------
We keep a FIFO queue ACTIVE that stores exactly those sites whose
y-coordinate is within 1 of the site we are about to process.

Algorithm ClosestPairWidth2(L)   // L is sorted by increasing y
    ACTIVE ← ∅                        // queue, ordered by y
    for i ← 1 to n do                  // scan in y–order
        while ACTIVE ≠ ∅ and ACTIVE.front.y < yi – 1 do
              ACTIVE.pop_front()      // site is too far below
        for each point q ∈ ACTIVE do  // ≤ 8 iterations, see (*)
              if (xi – qx)^2 + (yi – qy)^2 < 1 then
                     return “viability violated’’
        ACTIVE.push_back(li)
    return “all launch-sites viable”

----------------------------------------------------
3 Correctness proof
----------------------------------------------------
We prove that the algorithm outputs “viability violated’’
iff ∃ i ≠ j with ‖li – lj‖ < 1.

(⇒) If the algorithm outputs “violated’’ it has explicitly found a pair
(li , q) whose Euclidean distance it computed to be < 1.  
Hence the output is correct.

(⇐) Assume there exists such a pair li , lj .
W.l.o.g. Yi < Yj ; while scanning, we reach lj second.
Because ‖li – lj‖ < 1, inequality (1) gives
Yj – Yi < 1, so when lj is processed,
li is still inside ACTIVE (the queue only drops points whose y is
≥ 1 below the current one).
During the inner loop lj is compared with every member of ACTIVE,
hence in particular with li , and the algorithm reports the violation.

----------------------------------------------------
4 Running-time analysis
----------------------------------------------------
The outer for-loop executes n times.
By property (*)  the inner loop executes at most 8 iterations,
each costing O(1).  
All queue operations are O(1).   
Therefore the total running time is Θ(n).

----------------------------------------------------
5 Space usage
----------------------------------------------------
ACTIVE never stores more than 8 points, so the algorithm needs O(1)
auxiliary space.

----------------------------------------------------
6 Result
----------------------------------------------------
We have an O(n)-time, O(1)-space algorithm that, given the sites sorted
by y-coordinate and confined to –1 ≤ x ≤ 1, determines whether any two
sites lie within Euclidean distance 1 of each other.

\boxed{\text{Scan the points in increasing }y,\; keep a 1\text{-unit
vertical window that contains }\le 8\text{ sites};\;
compare the current point with those }\le 8\text{ sites.}}

Confidence: 0.52
